content.text: What Is Pass-Through Authentication

Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) is a hybrid authentication method where Entra ID validates user credentials against on-premises Active Directory in real time. Unlike Password Hash Synchronization, no credential derivative is stored in Entra ID. Instead, Entra ID passes the validation request to a lightweight on-premises agent, which performs an LDAP bind or Kerberos authentication against a domain controller and returns the result.

The PTA agent establishes a persistent outbound HTTPS connection to Entra ID's Service Bus. When a sign-in request arrives, Entra routes the encrypted credential validation to an available agent over this existing channel. No inbound firewall ports are required on the on-premises network.

attrs.alt: PTA sign-in flow: Entra routes encrypted validation request to on-premises agent, agent authenticates against AD DC

content.text: PTA Agent Installation and High Availability

The PTA agent is a small Windows service that connects outbound to Azure Service Bus. Microsoft recommends deploying at least three agents on different servers for high availability. Entra ID load-balances sign-in requests across all healthy agents. If an agent goes offline, the remaining agents handle the load without any configuration change.

# Install PTA agent on an on-premises Windows Server
# Prerequisites: Windows Server 2016+, .NET 4.7.2+, outbound HTTPS to:
#   *.msappproxy.net, *.servicebus.windows.net (port 443)
#   login.microsoftonline.com, login.windows.net (port 443)

# Download the agent from Entra portal:
# Entra ID > Security > Hybrid > Download On-premises Authentication Agent

# After installing the MSI, register the agent (run on the agent server):
# Start > Microsoft Azure AD Connect Authentication Agent
# Sign in with Hybrid Identity Administrator credentials

# Verify registration via Microsoft Graph
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Directory.Read.All'
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/onPremisesPublishingProfiles/authentication/agents' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Select-Object machineName, status, externalIp, appVersion, createdDateTime |
    Format-Table -AutoSize

# Agent statuses:
#   active   - healthy, processing requests
#   inactive - registered but not connected (check service, network)

# Check agent Windows service status on the agent server
Get-Service 'AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgent' | Select-Object Name, Status, StartType

# View agent event log
Get-EventLog -LogName Application -Source 'AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgent' -Newest 20 |
    Select-Object TimeGenerated, EventID, EntryType, Message | Format-Table -Wrap
Deployment recommendation
Reason
3+ agents on separate servers
Automatic failover if one agent goes offline; no manual intervention
Agents on non-DC servers
Reduces attack surface; agent compromise does not equal DC access
Agents behind different network paths
Guards against single network segment failure
Agents on servers not running Entra Connect
Entra Connect already runs an embedded PTA agent; separate agent servers add HA
How Credential Validation Works

When a user submits credentials to Entra ID, the credentials are encrypted with the PTA agent's public key before being placed on the Service Bus queue. Only the agent holding the corresponding private key can decrypt and process the validation request. The agent performs a Windows LDAP bind with the supplied username and password against a local domain controller. The domain controller's response — success, wrong password, account locked, password expired, etc. — is relayed back to Entra ID.
Password Policies with PTA

Because PTA validates credentials against AD in real time, the user's AD password policy governs sign-in — including minimum length, complexity, history, and expiry. When a user's AD password expires, PTA validation returns a 'password expired' signal to Entra ID. Entra ID can then prompt the user to change their password via the cloud if SSPR with password writeback is enabled.

# Check password policy applied to a specific user in AD
Import-Module ActiveDirectory

$user = Get-ADUser 'alice' -Properties PasswordLastSet, PasswordNeverExpires,
    PasswordExpired, LockedOut, BadLogonCount, LastBadPasswordAttempt

$fgpp = Get-ADUserResultantPasswordPolicy $user
if ($fgpp) {
    Write-Output "Fine-Grained Policy: $($fgpp.Name)"
    Write-Output "  Min length: $($fgpp.MinPasswordLength)"
    Write-Output "  Lockout threshold: $($fgpp.LockoutThreshold)"
    Write-Output "  Max password age: $($fgpp.MaxPasswordAge)"
} else {
    $domain = Get-ADDomain
    Write-Output "Default domain policy:"
    Write-Output "  Max password age: $((Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy).MaxPasswordAge)"
    Write-Output "  Lockout threshold: $((Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy).LockoutThreshold)"
}

Write-Output ""
Write-Output "User state:"
Write-Output "  PasswordLastSet: $($user.PasswordLastSet)"
Write-Output "  PasswordExpired: $($user.PasswordExpired)"
Write-Output "  LockedOut: $($user.LockedOut)"
Write-Output "  BadLogonCount: $($user.BadLogonCount)"

# Unlock a user locked in AD (affects PTA validation immediately)
Unlock-ADAccount -Identity 'alice'

# Enable SSPR password change on expiry (requires password writeback)
# Users with expired passwords are redirected to the SSPR change-password flow
# in Entra ID after PTA returns the 'password expired' error code
PTA Security Considerations

PTA agents handle decrypted credentials on-premises. An attacker who compromises a PTA agent server can intercept cleartext credentials in memory. This risk is distinct from PHS, where the on-premises sync engine never processes plaintext credentials at authentication time. Hardening PTA agent servers is therefore a critical security control.

# PTA agent server hardening checklist (PowerShell verification)

# 1. Confirm no users are logged into the agent server interactively
query user

# 2. Verify Windows Defender or AV is active
Get-MpComputerStatus | Select-Object AMServiceEnabled, RealTimeProtectionEnabled, AntispywareEnabled

# 3. Confirm agent service runs under a dedicated service account (not SYSTEM)
Get-WmiObject Win32_Service -Filter "Name = 'AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgent'" |
    Select-Object Name, StartName, State

# 4. Verify Windows Firewall blocks all inbound connections (agent is outbound-only)
Get-NetFirewallProfile | Select-Object Name, Enabled, DefaultInboundAction
# DefaultInboundAction should be 'Block' on all profiles

# 5. Check that only required outbound ports are allowed
# Required outbound: TCP 443 to *.msappproxy.net, *.servicebus.windows.net
Test-NetConnection -ComputerName 'packetcapture.msappproxy.net' -Port 443

# 6. Confirm the server is joined to domain but not a DC
(Get-WmiObject Win32_ComputerSystem).DomainRole
# 0 = standalone workstation, 1 = member workstation, 2 = standalone server
# 3 = member server (desired), 4 = backup DC, 5 = primary DC

# 7. Verify certificate-based channel encryption between agent and Entra ID
Get-EventLog -LogName Application -Source 'AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgent' `
    -Newest 5 | Where-Object { $_.EventID -eq 1002 } |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty Message
# Should show 'Agent connected to service' with certificate thumbprint
Risk
Mitigation
Credential interception on agent server
Harden agent servers: AV, EDR, no interactive logons, Privileged Access Workstation practices
Agent server compromise
Enable PHS as backup auth; rotate agents by deploying new ones and removing compromised
Service Bus channel man-in-the-middle
All traffic is TLS 1.2+; agent validates Entra's certificate against a pinned CA
AD brute force via PTA
AD lockout policy applies; Entra Smart Lockout provides additional cloud-layer protection
Single point of failure
Deploy 3+ agents across separate servers and network segments
Switching Between PTA and PHS

You can change authentication method at any time from the Entra Connect wizard or via PowerShell. The switch takes effect immediately for new sign-ins. Existing tokens remain valid until they expire. Microsoft recommends enabling PHS as a backup for PTA environments — if all PTA agents go offline, Entra ID can fall back to PHS automatically without any manual intervention.

# Switch from PTA to PHS using Entra Connect PowerShell
Import-Module ADSync

# Check current authentication method
Get-ADSyncAADCompanyFeature | Select-Object PasswordHashSync, PassThroughAuthentication

# Enable PHS (keeps PTA running in parallel as a fallback or replaces it)
Set-ADSyncAADPasswordSyncConfiguration -SourceConnector 'contoso.com' `
    -TargetConnector 'Entra ID Connector' -Enable $true

# To switch entirely from PTA to PHS:
# 1. Ensure PHS is enabled and all hashes are synced
# 2. Change the sign-in method in Entra Connect wizard to 'Password Hash Synchronization'
# 3. PTA agents remain installed but become inactive for sign-in validation

# Verify the switch by checking sign-in logs
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AuditLog.Read.All'
$since = (Get-Date).AddMinutes(-30).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn -Filter "createdDateTime ge $since" -Top 10 |
    Select-Object CreatedDateTime, UserPrincipalName, AuthenticationDetails |
    ForEach-Object {
        $authMethod = $_.AuthenticationDetails[0]?.AuthenticationMethod
        "$($_.UserPrincipalName): $authMethod"
    }
# With PHS: AuthenticationMethod = 'Password'
# With PTA: AuthenticationMethod = 'Password' (same UI, different backend)
Troubleshooting PTA Sign-In Failures

PTA authentication failures appear in both the Entra ID sign-in logs (cloud perspective) and the PTA agent Windows event log (on-premises perspective). Correlating both views is essential because Entra reports the final error code while the agent log shows the AD-specific reason for the failure.

# Step 1: Check Entra ID sign-in logs for the failure
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AuditLog.Read.All'

$upn   = 'alice@contoso.com'
$since = (Get-Date).AddHours(-2).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')

Get-MgAuditLogSignIn `
    -Filter "userPrincipalName eq '$upn' and createdDateTime ge $since" `
    -Top 20 |
    Select-Object CreatedDateTime,
        @{N='ErrorCode';     E={$_.Status.ErrorCode}},
        @{N='FailureReason'; E={$_.Status.FailureReason}},
        @{N='IsInteractive'; E={$_.IsInteractive}},
        @{N='AgentIP';       E={$_.NetworkLocationDetails[0]?.NetworkNames -join ','}} |
    Format-Table -AutoSize

# Step 2: Correlate with agent event log (run on each agent server)
# Common event IDs and their meanings:
$eventMap = @{
    1005 = 'Auth request received'
    1006 = 'Auth SUCCESS'
    1007 = 'Auth FAIL: wrong password'
    1008 = 'Auth FAIL: account locked (AD lockout)'
    1009 = 'Auth FAIL: account disabled'
    1010 = 'Auth FAIL: password expired'
    1014 = 'Auth FAIL: user not found in AD'
    1017 = 'Agent cannot reach domain controller'
    1021 = 'Agent lost Service Bus connection'
}

Get-EventLog -LogName Application -Source 'AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgent' `
    -Newest 100 -EntryType Error, Warning, Information |
    Where-Object { $_.EventID -in $eventMap.Keys } |
    Select-Object TimeGenerated, EventID,
        @{N='Meaning'; E={$eventMap[$_.EventID]}},
        @{N='Detail';  E={$_.Message.Substring(0, [Math]::Min(120, $_.Message.Length))}} |
    Format-Table -Wrap

# Step 3: If agent cannot reach DC (event 1017), check DNS and network
Resolve-DnsName 'contoso.com' -Type SRV  # should return DC records
Test-NetConnection -ComputerName (Resolve-DnsName '_ldap._tcp.contoso.com' -Type SRV).NameTarget[0] -Port 389
Entra error code
PTA agent event
Root cause
Fix
AADSTS81007
Wrong password entered
User resets password or tries again
AADSTS50053
AD account locked out
Unlock-ADAccount in AD
AADSTS50057
AD account disabled
Enable-ADAccount in AD
AADSTS50055
AD password expired
User changes password; enable SSPR writeback
AADSTS81012
Agent cannot reach DC
Check DNS, network, and DC health
AADSTS50074
MFA required by CA
User completes MFA — not a PTA failure
No event
Agent lost Service Bus connection
Restart agent service; check outbound HTTPS
PTA with Seamless SSO

Seamless SSO works with PTA to provide silent sign-in for domain-joined devices within the corporate network. When a user on a domain-joined machine accesses a cloud app, their browser automatically presents a Kerberos ticket to Entra ID's autologon endpoint, bypassing the PTA credential validation entirely. PTA is only invoked when Seamless SSO is not available — for example, on unjoined devices or outside the corporate network.

# Verify Seamless SSO is enabled alongside PTA
Import-Module AzureADSSO
New-AzureADSSOAuthenticationContext
Get-AzureADSSOStatus

# Enable Seamless SSO on domains managed by PTA
Enable-AzureADSSO -Enable $true

# Confirm the AZUREADSSOACC computer account exists in AD
Get-ADComputer -Filter { Name -eq 'AZUREADSSOACC' } -Properties Description, Created

# Roll over the Kerberos key every 30 days
Update-AzureADSSOForest -OnPremCredentials (Get-Credential)

# Users need autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com in their Intranet zone
# Deploy via Group Policy:
# Computer Config > Admin Templates > Windows Components > Internet Explorer
# > Internet Control Panel > Security Page > Site to Zone Assignment List
# Value: https://autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com = 1 (Intranet zone)

# Confirm Seamless SSO is working: check sign-in logs
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AuditLog.Read.All'
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn -Filter 'isInteractive eq false' -Top 10 |
    Select-Object CreatedDateTime, UserPrincipalName,
        @{N='AuthMethod'; E={$_.AuthenticationDetails[0]?.AuthenticationMethod}},
        @{N='IsInteractive'; E={$_.IsInteractive}}
# isInteractive=false + no MFA prompt = Seamless SSO success
Monitoring PTA Health at Scale

In large deployments with many agents and high sign-in volumes, proactive monitoring of agent health and authentication latency prevents sign-in degradation before users notice it. The Microsoft Graph API exposes agent health data, and the Windows event log on each agent provides per-request timing.

# Build a PTA health dashboard using Graph
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Directory.Read.All','AuditLog.Read.All'

Write-Output '=== PTA AGENT HEALTH ==='
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/onPremisesPublishingProfiles/authentication/agents' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Select-Object machineName, status, externalIp, appVersion |
    Format-Table

Write-Output ''
Write-Output '=== SIGN-IN FAILURE SUMMARY (last 24h) ==='
$since = (Get-Date).AddHours(-24).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn `
    -Filter "createdDateTime ge $since and status/errorCode ne 0" `
    -Top 1000 |
    Group-Object { $_.Status.ErrorCode } |
    Sort-Object Count -Descending |
    Select-Object -First 10 |
    ForEach-Object {
        [PSCustomObject]@{
            ErrorCode = $_.Name
            Count     = $_.Count
            Sample    = $_.Group[0].Status.FailureReason
        }
    } | Format-Table

Write-Output ''
Write-Output '=== PTA LATENCY (99th percentile, last hour) ==='
# PTA latency is not directly exposed in Graph; use agent event log
# Event 1006 contains duration in milliseconds
# On each agent server:
$events = Get-EventLog -LogName Application -Source 'AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgent' `
    -After (Get-Date).AddHours(-1) | Where-Object EventID -eq 1006
$durations = $events | ForEach-Object {
    if ($_.Message -match 'in (\d+) ms') { [int]$Matches[1] }
} | Sort-Object
if ($durations) {
    $p99idx = [int]($durations.Count * 0.99)
    "P50: $($durations[[int]($durations.Count*0.5)]) ms"
    "P99: $($durations[$p99idx]) ms"
    "Max: $($durations[-1]) ms"
}
Summary

Pass-Through Authentication gives organizations real-time credential validation against on-premises AD without storing any credential derivative in Entra ID. This satisfies compliance requirements that prohibit cloud storage of credential data, while still enabling Conditional Access, MFA, and Identity Protection. The tradeoff is an on-premises dependency at every sign-in — PTA agents and AD domain controllers must be reachable for authentication to succeed.
PTA routes sign-in requests to on-premises agents via an outbound-only persistent HTTPS channel — no inbound ports required
Agents decrypt the validation request using their private key; credentials never travel outside the on-premises network unencrypted
Deploy 3+ agents on separate member servers (not DCs) for high availability and security isolation
AD password policies, lockout thresholds, and account states apply directly — changes take effect immediately at next sign-in
PTA agents are a security-sensitive surface; harden agent servers with EDR, no interactive logons, and minimal installed software
Enable PHS as a backup: if all PTA agents are offline, sign-in falls back to PHS automatically
Switch between PTA and PHS via the Entra Connect wizard; the change takes effect for new sign-ins immediately
